name: Food & Cooking
description: Savor questions on cuisines, chefs, and culinary history worldwide.

questions.text: What is the main ingredient in guacamole?
questions.explanation: Guacamole is made primarily from mashed avocados, originating from Aztec cuisine in Mexico.

questions.text: Which country is the origin of sushi?
questions.explanation: Sushi originated in Japan, evolving from a preservation method where fish was fermented with rice.

questions.text: What type of pasta is shaped like small rice grains?
questions.explanation: Orzo is a rice-shaped pasta whose name means 'barley' in Italian, commonly used in soups and salads.

questions.text: Which spice is known as 'saffron' and comes from a crocus flower?
questions.explanation: Saffron is harvested from Crocus sativus stigmas; it is the world's most expensive spice by weight.

questions.text: What is the French term for cooking food slowly in its own fat?
questions.explanation: Confit (French: 'preserved') is a technique where food is slow-cooked and stored in its own fat.

questions.text: Which nut is used to make marzipan?
questions.explanation: Marzipan is made primarily from ground almonds and sugar, used in confectionery and cake decoration.

questions.text: What is the main protein source in a traditional French bouillabaisse?
questions.explanation: Bouillabaisse is a Provençal fish stew from Marseille, traditionally made with various fish and shellfish.

questions.text: Which vitamin is primarily found in citrus fruits and prevents scurvy?
questions.explanation: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) prevents scurvy; British sailors were given limes to prevent the disease.

questions.text: What is the name of the Japanese rice wine often served warm?
questions.explanation: Sake is a Japanese alcoholic beverage made by fermenting polished rice, often served warm or chilled.

questions.text: Which cooking technique involves sealing food in airtight bags and cooking in water at precise temperatures?
questions.explanation: Sous vide (French: 'under vacuum') cooks food in sealed bags at precise, consistent temperatures in a water bath.

questions.text: What is the base spirit in a Margarita cocktail?
questions.explanation: A Margarita is made with tequila, triple sec, and lime juice, typically served with a salted rim.

questions.text: Which country is the largest producer of coffee in the world?
questions.explanation: Brazil has been the world's largest coffee producer for over 150 years, producing about 40% of global supply.

questions.text: What is 'umami' often described as?
questions.explanation: Umami is the fifth basic taste, described as savory or meaty, identified by Japanese chemist Kikunae Ikeda in 1908.

questions.text: Which chef is known for the Fat Duck restaurant in Bray, England?
questions.explanation: Heston Blumenthal is the chef-owner of The Fat Duck, famous for molecular gastronomy and multi-sensory dining.

questions.text: What grain is used to make traditional Italian polenta?
questions.explanation: Polenta is made from ground cornmeal (maize) and is a staple in northern Italian cuisine.

questions.text: What is the Maillard reaction in cooking?
options: The caramelization of sugar at high temperatures
A chemical reaction between amino acids and sugars that browns food
The emulsification of fat in water
The gelatinization of starch in liquid
questions.explanation: The Maillard reaction occurs between amino acids and reducing sugars when heated, creating browned, flavorful crusts.

questions.text: Which cheese is traditionally used in a Caprese salad?
questions.explanation: A Caprese salad is made with fresh mozzarella, tomatoes, and basil, representing the Italian flag's colors.

questions.text: Where did the croissant actually originate?
questions.explanation: The croissant is derived from the Austrian kipferl; it was introduced to France in the early 19th century.

questions.text: What does 'Al dente' mean when cooking pasta?
questions.explanation: Al dente means 'to the tooth' in Italian, describing pasta cooked so it still offers slight resistance when bitten.

questions.text: Which country does kimchi originate from?
questions.explanation: Kimchi is a traditional Korean dish of salted and fermented vegetables, most commonly napa cabbage and radish.

questions.text: What is the primary ingredient in hummus?
questions.explanation: Hummus is made from cooked, mashed chickpeas blended with tahini, lemon juice, and garlic.

questions.text: Which fermentation process is used to make sourdough bread?
options: Wild yeast and lactic acid bacteria fermentation
questions.explanation: Sourdough relies on wild yeast and lactic acid bacteria in a starter culture to leaven and flavor the bread.

questions.text: What spice blend is essential to a traditional Indian curry?
questions.explanation: Garam masala is a blend of ground spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, cumin, etc.) fundamental to Indian cooking.

questions.text: How many calories are in one gram of fat?
questions.explanation: Fat provides 9 calories per gram, more than double that of protein or carbohydrates (both 4 cal/g).

questions.text: What classic French sauce is made from butter, white wine, and shallots?
questions.explanation: Beurre blanc (French: 'white butter') is an emulsified butter sauce made with a white wine and shallot reduction.

questions.text: Which chef wrote 'Mastering the Art of French Cooking', popularizing French cuisine in America?
questions.explanation: Julia Child co-wrote Mastering the Art of French Cooking (1961) and later hosted The French Chef on TV.

questions.text: What is the traditional wood used to smoke Texas-style BBQ brisket?
questions.explanation: Post oak is the traditional wood for Central Texas BBQ, prized for its clean, mild smoke that doesn't overpower beef.

questions.text: Which country is known for inventing pizza?
questions.explanation: Pizza originated in Naples, Italy; the classic Margherita was allegedly named after Queen Margherita in 1889.

questions.text: What is 'tempering' chocolate?
options: Melting chocolate slowly in a double boiler
A process of controlled heating and cooling to stabilize cocoa butter crystals
Adding cream to chocolate to make ganache
Whipping chocolate with air to lighten it
questions.explanation: Tempering chocolate stabilizes cocoa butter crystals through controlled temperature cycles, giving chocolate a glossy snap.

questions.text: What is the world's most consumed fish?
questions.explanation: Alaska pollock is the world's most consumed fish by volume, used widely in fish sticks, fish sandwiches, and surimi.

questions.text: Which fermented Korean soybean paste is a staple condiment similar to Japanese miso?
questions.explanation: Doenjang is a Korean fermented soybean paste with a stronger, more pungent flavor than Japanese miso.

questions.text: Which country is the largest producer of olive oil in the world?
questions.explanation: Spain is the world's largest producer of olive oil, accounting for roughly 40-45% of global production.

questions.text: What is the primary ingredient in a traditional Thai green curry paste?
questions.explanation: Thai green curry paste gets its color and heat from fresh green chilies, along with lemongrass, galangal, and herbs.

questions.text: What cooking method uses dry heat from all directions in an enclosed space?
questions.explanation: Both roasting and baking use dry oven heat; roasting typically refers to meat/vegetables, baking to breads/pastries.

questions.text: Which French chef is widely considered the father of modern cooking?
questions.explanation: Auguste Escoffier modernized and codified French cuisine in the late 19th century, creating the brigade kitchen system.

questions.text: What is 'Wagyu' beef?
options: A cut of beef from Scotland
A Japanese breed known for high marbling and fat content
questions.explanation: Wagyu refers to several Japanese cattle breeds with intense intramuscular fat marbling, producing exceptionally tender beef.

questions.text: What is the traditional grain used in risotto?
questions.explanation: Arborio (and other short-grain varieties like Carnaroli) releases starch during cooking to create risotto's creamy texture.

questions.text: Which enzyme in pineapple breaks down protein and tenderizes meat?
questions.explanation: Bromelain is a protease found in pineapple that breaks down protein, making it an effective meat tenderizer.

questions.text: What is Worcestershire sauce traditionally made from?
questions.explanation: Worcestershire sauce contains fermented anchovies, malt vinegar, molasses, tamarind, onion, and spices.

questions.text: Which nut is toxic to dogs and causes neurological symptoms?
questions.explanation: Macadamia nuts are toxic to dogs, causing weakness, vomiting, tremors, and hyperthermia; the mechanism is unknown.

questions.text: What is the traditional alcoholic base of a classic Tiramisu?
questions.explanation: Traditional Italian Tiramisu uses Marsala wine (and sometimes espresso-soaked ladyfingers with mascarpone cream).

questions.text: What gives Béarnaise sauce its distinctive anise-like flavor?
questions.explanation: Béarnaise sauce is flavored with tarragon and chervil, giving it a distinctive herbal and slightly anise-like taste.

questions.text: In which century was chocolate introduced to Europe from the Americas?
questions.explanation: Chocolate was brought to Europe in the 16th century by Spanish conquistadors who encountered it in Mesoamerica.

questions.text: What is the Indian bread that is cooked directly on a flame or open fire?
questions.explanation: Chapati (roti) is an unleavened flatbread cooked on a tawa and finished directly over a flame to puff up.

questions.text: Which region of France is famous for producing Champagne?
questions.explanation: Champagne can only be legally produced in the Champagne region of northeastern France using specific methods.

questions.text: What is the name of the traditional Ethiopian flatbread used as both plate and utensil?
questions.explanation: Injera is a sourdough flatbread made from teff flour; food is placed on it and eaten by tearing pieces to scoop dishes.

questions.text: Which culinary herb is also known as 'the king of herbs'?
questions.explanation: Basil is called 'the king of herbs' (from Greek basilikón phutón: 'royal plant') and is fundamental to Italian cuisine.

questions.text: What does 'blanching' a vegetable involve?
options: Cooking at very high heat in a wok
Briefly boiling then immediately plunging into ice water
questions.explanation: Blanching involves brief boiling then shocking in ice water to stop cooking, preserving color, texture, and nutrients.

questions.text: Which famous chef said 'Cooking is one of the oldest arts'?
questions.explanation: Jean Anthelme Brillat-Savarin, author of 'The Physiology of Taste' (1825), is one of history's most celebrated food writers.

questions.text: What is the name of the process that gives pretzels their distinctive chewy crust?
options: Dipping in lye (sodium hydroxide) solution before baking
questions.explanation: Traditional pretzels are dipped in a food-grade lye (sodium hydroxide) solution, which causes the Maillard reaction and creates their chewy, glossy crust.
